When these guys said to me, oh, there's so many objections, we can't even get our pitch off. So watch, there's so many objections, there's thousands of objections. Okay, fair enough, right? So I, that triggered me to start really, there's that many objections, great, let's write them all down. And the reason I said that to them that night was because I was what I was intending to do was I was going to write them all down. And then I was going to do this, you know, one by one, bam, bam, I was going to go and I used to get in a row, I just give me any objection, I'll just bam, I'll show you how I plow through it, right? And that was my intent. So I was gonna have them write down, you know, all these objections that I really had never thought about how many there were at that point in my life, I was, you know, my early 20s, right? So write them all down and I was going to go on a fucking 30, 40 minute roll, rapid fire, overcoming objections, right? But the second thing they said had stuck in my craw of that meaning we can't even get our pitches off. So there's things with so many objections, all right, fine, fair enough. We can't get our pitches off, independent thoughts. Why? Because here's the deal. When do you typically get hit with objections? After you ask for the order, right? Think about it. Why would someone hit you with an objection before you even asked them to buy something, except in the first moment that you basically, hi, you know, John, reasonable, I'm too busy, sorry, that's an objection, right? I have time right now. Okay, but with real objections, common objections, you get hit with them after you say here's a like you to do, pick up 10,000 shares or buy this car or buy this insurance policy or this is the TV I think you should own, this first doesn't matter what you're selling, right? You get hit with that first objection when you say here's what I'd like you to do, sign the contract and they say it sounds good, you want to let me think about it. That's basic logic. Yet my guys had said that we can't get our presentations off because we're getting hit with objections all along the way. I was like, what? I was like, just think about that. And I was like, wait a second, I never get hit with objections. In other words, once I was on that phone, I took control of the conversation and I didn't even think of it like that back before that night. I was just, you know, I just went on the phone was being me, I was being, you know, I was just being awesome on the phone, I thought it was happening, right? But because of that, right, I would, I was essentially being, it wasn't like I was taking control. So look at it differently. I was being handed control. They were seating control to me, because we've been taught since we're a big that we're in the position of experts, figures of authority, we defer, we listen, we don't interrupt from our teachers who stood before us in school to the doctors who helped us get better to the lawyers we met later on in life and everyone in between, even to the police officer, okay, a figure of authority, right, you defer, right. And by the way, and you'll say, well, yeah, of course, you defer to a police officer, you know, he's got a gun. If you don't defer, he'll shoot you early, throw you in jail. But that's not entirely true, is it? See, authority is perceived. Let me give you an example. You ever go to like a shopping mall and you have one of these toy cops, like a rent-a-cop, right, they're not real cops, right, but they got a badge, and they have like, you know, a little flashlight. And you know, you're in if you do they'll say, excuse me, you need to move your car and your department, you know, so what do you do? You move your car? Why? Not because what's he is a toy cop, the guy, but you still comply because you're like, he's a figure of authority, he's got a badge, he's got a uniform, and that hits a spot in us a pattern, we say, oh, figure of authority defer, listen, follow instructions, you get it? So that's why we'll defer to perceived figures of authority. So police officer, yeah, I'll lock you up and shoot you, but no, even someone that's just a figure of security guard, who you probably know is either a closet rapist or went to jail himself at some point and done, I don't mean that, like, he called us rapists. Anyway, I'm just kidding. All right, the point is, is that we defer no matter what, because it's based on the perception of authority.
